content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

July 11, 2007

David Gordon
Burbank City Councilmember
851 N. Hollywood Way
Burbank, California 91505-2814

RE: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-07-104

Dear Mr. Gordon:

This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Because you do not seek advice regarding a specific governmental decision, we can provide you only informal assistance. Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c), copy enclosed.)

QUESTION

May you participate in city council decisions related to repairs, replacement or maintenance of existing tree wells and replacement of vegetation in or around pedestrian rights-of-way, when you own a professional office building directly in front of or adjacent to parkway trees?

CONCLUSION

You may participate in city council decisions related to repairs, replacement or maintenance of existing tree wells and replacement of vegetation in or around pedestrian rights-of-way, as long as there is no reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on your economic interests as discussed below.

FACTS

You currently serve on Burbank's City Council. The council will soon be considering various proposals that concern either removal, planting, and repairs or

¹ Government Code sections 81000-91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. I-07-104
Page No. 2

maintenance of related infrastructure around trees and other vegetation located within the
city's pedestrian rights-of-way.

All of the trees involved in the proposals line major streets often referred to as
"major" or "secondary" arterials. They are located along "strip" commercial zones or
corridors and within the pedestrian sidewalks/public rights-of-way.

The proposed changes may occur only in certain districts within the City. For
example, the area where your small business and owner-occupied professional office
building is located is called the "Magnolia Park District" and encompasses several square
miles. The project might also include installing new protective tree grates or vegetation
within and along the public rights-of-way throughout the Magnolia Park District. Some
of the parkway trees and related items that will be considered by the Council are located
directly in front of, or adjacent to, your privately owned office building property, as well
as that of another newly elected councilmember.

You provided additional information in an electronic message on July 2, 2007,
stating that the proposal "mainly has to do with tree wells. If the wells are too small, the
aggressive tree roots tend to lift and break up the concrete causing safety issues and
expensive repairs. In some locations, very large open tree wells have been created to deal
with this situation. However, the large open wells create other problems with
pedestrians tripping, loose/blown soil, etc." You also stated that modifications to the tree
wells and possible replacement of trees are intended to "create a more pedestrian and
maintenance friendly and safe environment."

Your city attorney has suggested that you contact the FPPC to seek an opinion
before participating in the council agenda item. You wish to know whether you may
participate in any council consideration or deliberation related to public property/street
trees. If you may participate, you wish to know what limitations there may be on your
participation.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 of the Act prohibits a public official from making, participating in
making or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental
decision in which the official has a financial interest. In order to determine whether the
prohibition in section 87100 applies to a given decision, Regulation 18700 provides the
following eight-step analysis.

Steps One and Two: (1) Are you a "public official" and (2) are you making,
participating in making, or influencing a governmental decision?

The first two steps in the analysis are not at issue in your letter. As a city council
member, you are a "member, officer, employee, or consultant of a state or local

File No. I-07-104
Page No. 3

government agency" and, therefore, are a "public official" subject to the conflict-of-
interest provisions of the Act.² (Section 82048; Regulation 18701(a).) Additionally, you
will be making, participating in making or influencing a governmental decision if you
make recommendations to the city council or become involved in decisions related to
removal, planting, and improving infrastructure around various trees and other vegetation
located within the city's pedestrian rights-of-way. (Regulations 18702.1 and 18702.2.)

Step Three: What are your economic interests that might be a source of a
conflict of interest?

This step involves identifying the economic interests that may be impacted by a
governmental decision. Section 87103 and Regulation 18703 identifies the economic
interests that are used to determine if a governmental decision may result in a conflict of
interest for a public official. The economic interests pertinent to your question are as
follows:

1. Real Property: You have an economic interest in real property in which you,
your spouse, your registered domestic partner, or your dependent children or anyone
acting on your behalf has invested $2,000 or more; or if any of those persons has a
leasehold interest in real estate worth $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b), Regulation
18703.2.)

In your letter, you state that you own a professional office building in the city's
Magnolia Park District from which you operate a small business. For purposes of this
analysis, we assume that your investment in the real property is $2,000 or more,
therefore, you have an economic interest in real property.

2. Business Entity Investment, Employment or Management: An official has an
economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or indirect
investment of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(a); Regulation 18703.1(a)); or in which he
or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of
management (Section 87103(d); Regulation 18703.1(b)).

In your letter, you state that you own a small business in the city's Magnolia Park
District. For purposes of this analysis, we assume you have an investment of $2,000 or
more in your small business. If so, you have an economic interest in a business entity. In

² Recusal Requirements: If a public official is enumerated in section 87200 (including city
council members) and he or she has a conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, then he
or she must: (1) immediately prior to the discussion of the item, orally identify each type of economic
interest involved in the decision as well as details of the economic interest, as discussed in regulation
18702.5(b), on the record of the meeting; (2) recuse himself or herself; and (3) leave the room for the
duration of the discussion and/or vote on the item. For closed sessions, consent calendars, absences and
speaking as a member of the public regarding personal interests, special rules found in regulation 18702.5,
subdivisions (c) and (d) apply. (Section 87105.)

File No. I-07-104
Page No. 4

addition, if you are a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or hold any position of
management in this business, you have an economic interest in the business by virtue of
your business position. (Section 87103(d).)

3. Sources of Income/Clients: An official also has an economic interest in any
person from whom he or she has received income aggregating $500 within 12 months
preceding the relevant governmental decision. (Section 87103(c); Regulation 18703.3.)

You have not identified particular clients of yours who qualify as sources of
income, or who have provided you with income of greater than $500 within the 12-month
period leading to the governmental decision at issue here. We therefore do not include
them further in our analysis but point out that you must still determine whether any of
your clients qualify as a source of income and whether a conflict of interest will arise
from any of them (i.e., whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the governmental
decision you participate in will result in a material financial effect on any of these clients
who are economic interests of yours).

4. Personal Finances: A public official also has an economic interest in his
personal finances and those of his immediate family. (Regulation 18703.5.) "A
governmental decision will have an effect on this economic interest if the decision will
result in the personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities of the official or his or her
immediate family increasing or decreasing." (Ibid.) A reasonably foreseeable financial
effect on a public official's personal finances is material if it is at least $250 in any
12-month period.³ (Regulation 18705.5)

Your letter does not mention any impact on your personal finances as the result of
the upcoming decisions. However, please note that a conflict of interest may arise if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decisions will have a material effect on your personal
finances.

Step Four: Are these economic interests directly or indirectly involved in the
governmental decision?

Real Property Interest:

Ordinarily, real property located within 500 feet of the boundaries (or the
proposed boundaries) of the property that is the subject of a governmental decision is
directly involved in the decision. Accordingly, your economic interest in real property
that is within 500 feet of the project area would be considered directly involved in the
governmental decision unless an exception applies. (Regulation 18704.2(a)(1).)

³ Under regulation 18705.5, when determining whether a governmental decision has a material
financial effect on a public official's economic interest in his or her personal finances, neither a financial
effect on the value of real property owned directly or indirectly by the official, nor a financial effect on the
gross revenues, expenses, or value of assets and liabilities of a business entity in which the official has a
direct or indirect investment interest shall be considered.

File No. I-07-104
Page No. 5

However, Regulation 18704.2(b)(2) provides an exception that, notwithstanding
Regulation 18704.2(a), real property is indirectly involved in decisions that solely
concern "repairs, replacement, or maintenance of existing streets, water, sewer, storm
drainage, or similar facilities."

As you have described it, the proposal appears to consist of repairing, replacing or
maintaining existing tree wells and possibly replacing vegetation in or around pedestrian
rights-of-way. Based on this information, it appears that the primary purpose of the
project is for "repairs, replacement or maintenance" by the city to make these areas
"more pedestrian and maintenance friendly" and to increase safety in these areas.
Therefore, so long as the project is limited to repairs, replacement or maintenance of
existing facilities, your commercial property is deemed indirectly involved.

Business Entity, Source of Income:

A person, including business entities, sources of income and sources of gifts, is
directly involved in a decision before an official's agency when that person, either
directly or by an agent:

"(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by
filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or; (2) Is a named
party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding concerning the decision before
the official or the official's agency. A person is the subject of a
proceeding if a decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial
or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract
with, the subject person." (Regulation 18704.1(a).)

Your small business does not appear to be a named party in, nor is the subject of
proceedings concerning the proposed project, thus your business interest is indirectly
involved in the decisions.

Personal Finances:

If there is any financial effect on the personal finances of a public official or his or
her immediate family they are deemed to be directly involved in a decision. (Regulation
18704.5.) Because you have not presented any facts indicating a personal financial
effect, we do not analyze this factor.

Steps Five and Six: Will the financial effect of the decision on your economic
interest be material, and reasonably foreseeable?

Once a public official identifies his or her relevant economic interests, the official
must evaluate whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material

File No. I-07-104
Page No. 6

financial effect on one of those economic interests by applying the applicable materiality
standard set forth in Commission regulations. (Regulations 18700(b)(5) and 18705,
et seq.) After finding the applicable materiality standard, the official must then decide
whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the standard will be met. (Regulation
18700(b)(6).)

Real Property Interest:

As stated above, under Regulation 18704.2(a), the real property in which you
have an interest is considered indirectly involved in the governmental decisions. Under
Regulation 18705.2(b), the financial effect of a governmental decision that is indirectly
involved is presumed not to be material. However, the presumption may be rebutted by
"proof that there are specific circumstances regarding the governmental decision, its
financial effect, and the nature of the real property in which the public official has an
economic interest, which make it reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect on the real property in which the public official has an interest."
(Regulation 18705.2(b)(1).)

Examples of these specific circumstances include circumstances where the
decision affects:

"(A) The development potential or income producing potential of the real
property in which the official has an economic interest;

"(B) The use of the real property in which the official has an economic
interest;

"(C) The character of the neighborhood including, but not limited to,
substantial effects on: traffic, view, privacy, intensity of use, noise levels,
air emissions, or similar traits of the neighborhood."

Circumstances such as those listed above can rebut the presumption.
(Regulation 18705.2(b)(1)(A)-(C).) Therefore, you may participate in the
governmental decisions concerning repairs, replacement, or maintenance of existing
tree wells and replacement of vegetation in or around the city's pedestrian rights-of-
way as long as the decisions do not have a material financial effect on your real
property as discussed above.

Business Entity, Source of Income:

The financial effect of a governmental decision on a small business entity, which
is not directly involved in the decision, is material if it is reasonably foreseeable that:

File No. I-07-104
Page No. 7

"(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase or
decrease in the business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the
amount of $ 20,000 or more; or,

"(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity
incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating
existing expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $ 5,000 or more; or,

"(C) The governmental decision will result in an increase or
decrease in the value of the business entity's assets or liabilities of
$20,000 or more." (Regulation 18705.1(c)(4).)

This materiality standard is applicable to your small business, which is also a
source of income to you. A material financial effect on an economic interest is
reasonably foreseeable if it is substantially likely that one or more of the materiality
standards are applicable to that economic interest will be met as a result of the governmental
decision. (Regulation 18706(a).) Please note that the Commission does not act as a
finder of fact in providing advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71) Therefore, the
determination of whether or not it is reasonably foreseeable that the applicable materiality
standard will be met for your small business is necessarily a factual question that is
ultimately for you to decide.

Steps Seven and Eight: The "Public Generally" and "Legally Required
Participation" Exceptions

An official who otherwise has a conflict of interest in a decision may still
participate under the "public generally" exception. This exception applies when the
financial effect of a decision on a public official's economic interests is substantially the
same as the effect on a significant segment of the public. (See Section 87103;
Regulations 18707-18707.10.)

The "legally required participation" rule applies when the official's participation
in a governmental decision is legally required. (Section 87101; Regulation 18708.)

You have not presented any facts indicating that these exceptions are applicable to
your situation. Therefore, we do not address them here. Without further information
regarding the specific decisions that may arise, we are unable to render specific advice.
We trust, however, that the discussion above will help you identify the areas and
circumstances under which a conflict might arise with respect to your situation.

File No. I-07-104
Page No. 8




If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,


Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
[signature]
By: Emelyn Rodriguez
Counsel, Legal Division

ER:jgl

Enclosures

adjacent to parkway trees?

You may participate in city council decisions related to repairs, replacement, or
maintenance of existing tree wells and replacement of vegetation in or around pedestrian
rights-of-way, as long as there is no reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on
your economic interests as discussed below.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: May you participate in city council decisions related to repairs, replacement or
maintenance of existing tree wells and replacement of vegetation in or around pedestrian
rights-of-way, when you own a professional office building directly in front of or
adjacent to parkway trees?

CONCLUSION: You may participate in city council decisions related to repairs, replacement, or
maintenance of existing tree wells and replacement of vegetation in or around pedestrian
rights-of-way, as long as there is no reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on
your economic interests as discussed below.
embedding.first_500_words: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 July 11, 2007 David Gordon Burbank City Councilmember 851 N. Hollywood Way Burbank, California 91505-2814 RE: Your Request for Informal Assistance Our File No. I-07-104 Dear Mr. Gordon: This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Because you do not seek advice regarding a specific governmental decision, we can provide you only informal assistance. Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c), copy enclosed.) QUESTION May you participate in city council decisions related to repairs, replacement or maintenance of existing tree wells and replacement of vegetation in or around pedestrian rights-of-way, when you own a professional office building directly in front of or adjacent to parkway trees? CONCLUSION You may participate in city council decisions related to repairs, replacement or maintenance of existing tree wells and replacement of vegetation in or around pedestrian rights-of-way, as long as there is no reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on your economic interests as discussed below. FACTS You currently serve on Burbank's City Council. The council will soon be considering various proposals that concern either removal, planting, and repairs or ¹ Government Code sections 81000-91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations. File No. I-07-104 Page No. 2 maintenance of related infrastructure around trees and other vegetation located within the city's pedestrian rights-of-way. All of the trees involved in the proposals line major streets often referred to as "major" or "secondary" arterials. They are located along "strip" commercial zones or corridors and within the pedestrian sidewalks/public rights-of-way. The proposed changes may occur only in certain districts within the City. For example, the area where your small business and owner-occupied professional office building is located is called the "Magnolia Park District" and encompasses several square miles. The project might also include installing new protective tree grates or vegetation within and along the public rights-of-way throughout the Magnolia Park District. Some of the parkway trees and related items that will be considered by the Council are located directly in front of, or adjacent to, your privately owned office building property, as well as that of another newly elected councilmember. You provided additional information in an electronic message on July 2, 2007, stating that the proposal "mainly has to do with tree wells. If the wells are too small, the aggressive tree roots tend to lift and break up the concrete causing safety issues and expensive repairs. In some locations, very large open tree wells have been created to deal with this situation. However, the large open wells create other problems with pedestrians tripping, loose/blown soil, etc." You also stated that modifications to the tree wells and possible replacement of trees are intended to "create a more pedestrian and maintenance friendly and safe
